coredata.dc:title: Transformation of crystal structure induced by the temperatures in carbon dots (CDs)-based composites with multicolor fluorescence for white Light-Emitting-Diode (WLED)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Fengli
Dong, Ruoyu
Cui, Junchao
Zhang, Yufei
Ren, Weijie
Song, Kai
Meng, Shuai
Zhang, Rui
Li, Kun
Bai, Jingjing
Qin, Zhenxing
coredata.dc:description: Regulation of the fluorescence through crystalizing from the matrix in the Carbon dots (CDs)-based solid-state materials has been verified to be one of the effective methods, yet there are not only challenges in preparing such materials efficiently, but also insufficient insight into their regulation mechanisms. Here, a one-pot solvothermal route to synthesize a series of CDs-based composites with crystalline matrix is reported. These crystals exhibited multicolor fluorescence with the feature of multi-peaks emissions with increasing temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃, in which the orange emitting O-CDs@PA and the yellow emitting Y-CDs@PA crystals obtained the FLQYs of 22% and 68% respectively due to relatively stable crystalline structures. After comparative analysis to both crystals in detail, the core and the groups associated with them on the interface between CDs and matrix were adjusted in size and species during structural transformation of the crystal matrix, which changes radically the energy band structures to influence fluorescent emitting of both crystals ultimately. In addition, the reasons resulting in higher FLQY for Y-CDs@PA were provided leveraging the schematic illustration presumed based on the PL properties of both crystals. Because of the optimal optical performances, these fluorescent materials promised to fabricate WLED devices and obtained a number of photometric parameters endowed these WLED devices with the feature of warm-white light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-based composite
Crystal structure
Phase transformation
Multi-peaks
WLED device

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CDs@PA-based films and LED devices
Structure and optical property of the CDs@PA crystal under temperatures
Morphology, structure and optical property of the O- and Y-CDs@PA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work in China was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51502189 ), the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Provinces ( 202103021224268 , 20210302123221 , 202203021221146 , 202203021222207 , 202203021212332 ), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( 2022L320 , 2023L176 ), the Special Fund for Science and Technology Innovation Teams of Shanxi Province ( 202204051001002 ), and the Graduate Education Innovation Project ( SY2023057 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of carbon dots composites with multicolor fluorescence from PA and TU via one-pot solvothermal method under different conditions to in situ embed the carbon dots into a crystal matrix.
XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of the carbon dots composites (CDs@PA) at the temperatures of 140–220 ℃.
Normalized absorption (a), PLE (b) and PL spectra (c) of the carbon dots composites at the temperatures of 140–220 ℃. The dashed line indicates the change in peak position.
TEM images, HRTEM images (the inset on the top right corner) and corresponding statistical histograms of particle size (the inset on the bottom right corner) of O-CDs@PA (a) and Y-CDs@PA (b).

Indexing XRD patterns (a), TG/DTG analyses (b) of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA. XPS spectra (c), high-resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of the C 1 s (d, h), O 1 s (e, i), N 1 s (f, j) and S 2p (g, k) of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA.
UV–Vis absorption and excitation spectra (a), 3D fluorescence spectra (b, c) and fluorescence decay curves (d) of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA.

Schematic illustration of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA for their structure and luminescence mechanism of highly efficient crystal-state emission with the feature of multi-peaks. (IC: internal conversion, VR: vibration relaxation, the arrows of different colors represent the luminescence colors generated by the transitions between different energy levels, the thickness of the arrows indicates the intensity of the luminescence, and the gray arrows indicate no luminescence.).

The emitting light spectrum of the 1-WLED (a), 2-WLED (b), and 3-WLED (c) at a current of 20 mA, inset at right top corner is the optical photograph of the corresponding WLED at on-state. (d-f) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectrum of the corresponding WLED (a-c). (g-i) EL spectra of the corresponding WLED (a-c) in different currents as well as the CRI and CCT variations with increasing currents is shown in inset, respectively.
Content of various chemical bonds in O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA.
Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curves of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA powders at the different Emissions.

head.ce:title: Transformation of crystal structure induced by the temperatures in carbon dots (CDs)-based composites with multicolor fluorescence for white Light-Emitting-Diode (WLED)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Center of Technology Innovation for Light Manipulations and Applications, School of Applied Science, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, the People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Center of Technology Innovation for Light Manipulations and Applications
Taiyuan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Center of Technology Innovation for Light Manipulations and Applications, School of Applied Science, Taiyuan University of Science and Technology, Taiyuan 030024, the People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, the People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, the People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This figure shows a new crystal-engineered strategy for the controlled synthesis of CDs@PA composite with different colors by the structural transformation with increasing temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃. With the relatively stable crystalline structures, the orange emitting O-CDs@PA and the yellow emitting Y-CDs@PA crystals obtained the optimal optical properties, which mainly resulted from the redistribution of radiation recombination from the S1 and S2 energy levels because the core and the groups associated with them on the interface between CDs and matrix were adjusted during structural transformation of the crystal matrix.

Regulation of the fluorescence through crystalizing from the matrix in the Carbon dots (CDs)-based solid-state materials has been verified to be one of the effective methods, yet there are not only challenges in preparing such materials efficiently, but also insufficient insight into their regulation mechanisms. Here, a one-pot solvothermal route to synthesize a series of CDs-based composites with crystalline matrix is reported. These crystals exhibited multicolor fluorescence with the feature of multi-peaks emissions with increasing temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃, in which the orange emitting O-CDs@PA and the yellow emitting Y-CDs@PA crystals obtained the FLQYs of 22% and 68% respectively due to relatively stable crystalline structures. After comparative analysis to both crystals in detail, the core and the groups associated with them on the interface between CDs and matrix were adjusted in size and species during structural transformation of the crystal matrix, which changes radically the energy band structures to influence fluorescent emitting of both crystals ultimately. In addition, the reasons resulting in higher FLQY for Y-CDs@PA were provided leveraging the schematic illustration presumed based on the PL properties of both crystals. Because of the optimal optical performances, these fluorescent materials promised to fabricate WLED devices and obtained a number of photometric parameters endowed these WLED devices with the feature of warm-white light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterials, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention owing to their distinctive merits in the low-cost and eco-friendly preparation, photobleaching resistance and low toxicity, and widely available precursors,[1–5] thus, inspiring a large variety of applications in bioimaging[6], optical sensors[7] and anti-fakes[8], optoelectronic devices[9], catalysis[10,11] and energy conversion[12], and so forth. The CDs have, in other words, more striking advantages to simply obtain high-quality fluorescent products from the varied precursors compared with conventional luminescent materials. However, most CDs could undergo serious luminescence quenching when it was dryly solidified, known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, thereby, hindering their use as solid-state luminescent materials to fabricate LED devices and solar concentrators, etc.[13,14] To address this issue, the typical strategy for obtaining solid CDs is to incorporate the resultant CDs into the suitable matrices curable by some means such as starch[15], special functional material (Metal organic framework (MOF)[16], silica gel[17] and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES)[18], etc.), and polymer[19,20]. Nevertheless, it inevitably causes self-absorption and serious self-quenching along with a massive overdose of the CDs loaded into the matrix as a result of direct π-π interactions or resonance energy transfer (RET) between CDs monomers,[21,22] which not only greatly increases the complexity on the synthesis of solid-state CDs, but also seriously hinders the exploration of the optical regulations in such materials.

The pace to realizing the real optical device applications by fabricating solid-state fluorescence CDs is never quits. Recently, a facile approach to easily synthesize solid-state CDs by the crystallization of a matrix-materials has been reported and held a distinct advantage of regulating the luminous colors and fluorescence quantum yields (FLQYs). Qiu group facilely synthesized solid full-color phosphorescent CDs by the hydrothermal method of Citric acid and Boric acid, in which the fixation of B2O3 crystals in situ generated at high temperature (greater than 170℃) to the triplet state plays pivotal roles to phosphorescent emission of CDs composites.[23] Besides that, it also can efficiently inhibit nonradiative decay to promote optical performance in such CDs composite by tuning the interactions of hydrogen bonds and B-C bonds generated from the crystallization of the matrix in situ around CDs monomers[24] or inducing a transformation from a high‐energy amorphous state to another lower energy crystalline counterparts only via grinding manually bulk crystalline material to more evenly distribute CDs particle/cluster[25]. Accordingly, it seems that the crystallization of matrix utilizing Boric acid paves a way for CDs materials to achieve multicolor emissions with the extremely high FLQYs, yet Boric acid is not a great alternative in terms of the crystal material due to the harsh and unstable crystalline conditions which hinders further exploration of the effects of the crystal structures on optical properties in such solid-state nanomaterial. Coincidently, Jiang et al. synthesized another CDs composite with a crystallized matrix using Urea reagent, likewise, to realize the regulation of luminescence by triggering the covalent bonds between CDs inside and crystallized matrix of the Cyanuric acid.[26,27] Notably, the structure of matrix crystallized from Urea was more detailed and stable, thus setting the stage to directly solve the mechanism for customizing fluorescent emissions resulted from the crystallization and the conclusion indeed became more convictive, except for the relatively complicated preparation process which had to synthesize matrix-crystallized CDs composite via the two-step method. More encouragingly, Yang et al. fancifully prepared crystalline CDs without a matrix which was formed by the directly annular stacking of the CDs monomer after nucleating, thereby, promoting the optical properties by virtue of the efficient π-packing in crystal.[28] Given the above, in pursuit of a further insight into the fluorescence-emitted variations induced by the crystallization in such CDs materials and industrial application thereof, it still remains of a great challenge to fabricate the suitable products capable of achieving rich crystal structures as well as simplifying synthesized method of the solid-state CDs so far.

To address these suggestions, Phthalic acid reagent unexpectedly exhibited great potential in the crystallization under certain conditions when employed to nucleate into the CDs in the early work.[29] In addition, considering that element-doping has been an effective way to improve the optical properties of CDs,[30–32] the usage of element-dopant in the precursor will lay a solid foundation for CDs itself emissions to achieve more abundant colors. With these facts in mind, we developed a facile and simple strategy to in situ synthetize a series of the CDs-based fluorescence materials with multicolor emissions from crystalline thermal-annealed Phthalic acid and Thiourea as the dopant of S and N elements (CDs@PA) by the solvothermal method. With increasing reaction temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃, the resultant crystals emitted fluorescence with various colors under UV light and the variation of crystal structure was suggested to be underlying cause after comprehensive characterizations. As the typical crystals due to the relatively stable structures and outstanding emissions of the fluorescence, O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA synthesized at 160 ℃ and 220 ℃ respectively were explored to disclose the effects of the structural transformation on fluorescence, finally, which was found that collapsing of the core and variation of the groups on the interface between CDs and the matrix resulted from the phase transformation of the matrix structure together adjusted the energy levels, thereby, resulting in changes of the optical property in both crystals. With the features of multi-peaks and high FLQYs, these fluorescent crystals as the phosphors were also applied to the White Light-Emitting-Diode (WLED) devices and achieved excellent photometric parameters, which lays a solid foundation for these materials as a warm-white light to realize industrialization in lighting field, etc.

Phthalic acid (≥99.5 %, PA), Thiourea (≥99.0 %, TU) and NaOH were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). MeOH and PVP powder were supplied by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies Co., Ltd. All reagents and solvents were used without further purification. For the characterization of resultant samples, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on the JEOL/JEM-2100 of Japan. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images was performed on a JEOL JSM-6510 microscope with a primary electron energy of 3 kV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on an X-ray diffractometer (SmartLab 9 KW, Rigaku Co., Japan) equipped with Cu Ka radiation. X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a PHI-5000C ESCA system with Al Kα X-ray radiation (PerkinElmer Inc., USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained on a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer with the wavenumber range from 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 (Thermo Fisher, America). Thermogravimetric / derivative thermogravimetric analysis (TG/DTG) was collected in the temperature from room temperature to 800 ℃ at the heating rate of 30 ℃/min using the STA 449 F5 thermogravimetric analyzer (Jupiter, Germany). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a U-3900 UV/Visible spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Fluorescence spectra, lifetimes and quantum yields were measured using the FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer system (Edinburgh Instruments, England) at ambient conditions.

With the molar ratio of 2:1, 2:2, 1:2 and 0:2, PA and TU were poured into 10 ml MeOH solvent and sufficiently dissolved with the aid of ultrasound until it is transparent at the room temperature. Then, the precursors were moved into a Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 220 ℃ for 3 h, which obtained rod-shaped crystals emitting fluorescence with weak blue color and lightful yellow colors as well as no fluorescence (PA:TU = 2:1) after naturally cooled to the normal temperature. Prolonging the time to 6 h and 9 h respectively at the PA:TU = 1:2, the resultant products nearly remained the same yellow fluorescence, yet underwent a variation in fluorescent color along with the reaction temperature from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃ for 3 h. All of the products were again poured into a 50 ml beaker by the ultrasonication to elute unreacted components using 30 ml H2O in three times. At last, the rod-shaped solid-state precipitates were obtained after dried in an oven at 80 ℃ over 4 h and were grinded into exquisite powder for use on subsequent processes. In addition, only changed to H2O as solvent, it also obtained the CDs solution with blue fluorescence after performing the pyrolysis experiment of 1.0436 g PA and 0.9564 g TU (the molar ratio of PA:TU = 1:2) at the same conditions (220 ℃, 3 h). Furthermore, the resultant powder from these solutions had more superior blue fluorescence via the filtration using the membranes of 0.22 μm aperture to remove aggregated particles, dialysis to separate small unreacted molecules (1000 Da, 24 h) and freeze-drying processes, named as B-CDs@PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CDs@PA-based films and LED devices
ce:section.ce:para: 1 g PVP powders were firstly dissolved in 0.5 ml H2O to get PVP solution. Then, 0.2 g CDs@PA-based phosphor was mixed into the solution under the ultrasound of 5 min, which was quickly poured into teflon square mold after removing the air bubbles. Finally, the film with the corresponding fluorescence color was successfully obtained after drying naturally. For the LED devices, CDs@PA powders was mixed with UV structural adhesives (Shenzhen Tegu New materials Co., LTD) at the certain mass ratio, then, which was quickly dripped on the UV = 365 nm chip (San’an Optoelectronics Co., Ltd) to obtain the LED devices after cured at 150 ℃ for 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical property of the CDs@PA crystal under temperatures
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@PA crystals were synthsized by one-step solvothermal approach using PA and TU in MeOH solvent, which was described in detail in the Experimental Section and illustrated in Fig. 1 . Unsurprisedly, the final products are crystalline powder after grinding the rod-shaped fluorophor formed via hydrolysis-condensation reaction at the outset in view of the peculiarity of PA precursor. To improve the optical properties of CDs@PA crystals, molar ratio of the precursors was first optimized as a variate at the temperature of 220 ℃ and the heating time of 3 h, which ultimately obtained the outstanding yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light in the resultant products along with the PA:TU = 1:2 and 2:2. Additionally, the fluorescence seemed to remain constant when the heating time is prolonged to 6 h and 9 h. However, these CDs@PA crystals underwent a change on fluorescent color from yellow, green, cyan, orange and pink as the heating temperature decreased from 220 ℃ to 140 ℃ with the interval of 20 ℃. From the above phenomenon, one can see that CDs@PA crystals with multicolor fluorescence can be customized just by regulating the heating temperatures in this method.

To gain further insights into the effects of the heating temperature on the optical properties of the CDs@PA crystals, phase structures and functional groups of the CDs@PA crystals in the temperature range of 140–220 ℃ were characterized using XRD and FT-IR (Fig. 2 ). As shown in Fig. 2a, all of the CDs@PA composites prepared at different temperature presents a series of significant crystalline peaks, indicating to form homogeneous phase structures in these composites. Depending on the number of peaks in XRD spectra here, however, the CDs@PA crystal undergoes a phase transformation with the temperature up to 200 ℃ due to the emergence of new peaks (labeled with green star) and the reduction or even disappearance of several previous peaks (labeled with red star), which is finally stabilized at 220 ℃ resulted from shifting to another XRD pattern, suggesting that the resultant CDs@PA crystal at 220 ℃ possesses an entirely different crystalline phase in structure. For the CDs@PA crystals prepared in the temperature range of 140–180 ℃, there seems to be consistent crystalline phase because of the virtually same XRD patterns, but it is worthy to note that the above-mentioned phase transformation has already taken place in the CDs@PA crystal prepared at 180 ℃ based on the intensity analysis of partial characteristic peaks in the respective XRD pattern (Fig. S1 and Table S1), thus, resulted in ineligible to explore the crystalline structure using the crystal. In addition, there are a large number of the absorption peaks to change with the increasing temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃ in the FT-IR spectra of these crystals, which is also capable to indicate the phase transformation of the CDs@PA crystal dependent on temperature in a different way (Fig. 2b). Notably, the FT-IR spectra of the crystals prepared at 160 ℃ and 180 ℃ exhibit similar patterns in the whole region of 600–3600 cm−1. Compared to the case at the both temperatures, however, the spectrum of the CDs@PA crystal prepared at 140 ℃ is greatly different with the fine distinctions in the absorption regions of 3000–3600 cm−1, ca. 1600 cm−1, 1400–1500 cm−1, 1050–1200 cm−1, and 650–900 cm−1, thereby, providing evidence that the CDs@PA crystal prepared at 140 ℃ possesses a unique “CDs”, yet the similar crystal matrix to that at 160 ℃ as illustrated in the XRD patterns of Fig. 2a. Unsurprisingly, the FT-IR spectrum of the crystal prepared at 200 ℃ also reveals to undergo a transitional state on account of the emergence of new absorption peaks at the positions of 1433 cm−1, 1250 cm−1, 1007 cm−1 and 856 cm−1 (labeled with green star in Fig. 2b). Increasing temperature up to 220 ℃, the FT-IR spectrum of the resultant CDs@PA crystal ultimately presents a distinctive absorption pattern compared to that at 160 ℃. From what has been discussed above, the CDs@PA crystal are extremely susceptible to temperature during the synthesis, which not only induces directly a structural change of the crystal matrix, but also customizes the internal CDs in these crystals with variational temperatures.

Subsequently, optical properties of the CDs@PA crystals prepared at the temperatures of 140–220 ℃ are investigated by the UV–Vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 3 ). As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption spectra of the CDs@PA crystals at 180℃ and 200 ℃ are greatly different from the others, which should be resulted from the crystalline phase of both composites being in a transitional state with the disorder CDs structure inside the crystal matrix that is also undergoing a phase transition as indicated from the XRD and FT-IR results. Therefore, the corresponding excitation and emission spectra with distinctive pattern in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c is becoming understandable. The absorption spectrum of the CDs@PA crystal at 140 ℃ is similar to that at 160 ℃, only the relative intensities of characteristic peaks are not synchronous. As a result, excitation and emission spectra of the CDs@PA crystals at 140 ℃ and 160 ℃ obtain nearly consistent patterns in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c. Surprisingly, the absorption spectrum of the CDs@PA crystal at 220 ℃ seems to back to what it exhibits at 160 ℃ after undergoing a structural change thoroughly under temperatures of 180–200 ℃ which has been analyzed by the XRD in Fig. 2a. Suffering this effect, the emission spectrum also remains in accord with that at 160 ℃ except for having an overall blue-shift of the typical peaks slightly. Above all, it is apparent that the photoluminescence (PL) property of the CDs@PA crystals underwent similar variation rule to the structural transformation under temperatures, which fully suggests the importance of the crystalline structure to regulate optical performance of these materials. Furthermore, these crystals exhibited multicolor fluorescence with increasing temperatures from 140 ℃ to 220 ℃ as shown in Fig. 1, but only the CDs@PA crystals prepared at 160 ℃ with orange color and at 220 ℃ with yellow color presented bright emissions under 365 nm UV light in all products. Coincidingly, it is also known from the above that both crystals obtained the most stable and pure structure among these composites. Therefore, the CDs@PA crystals prepared at 160 ℃ and 220 ℃ will serve as models to further understand the correspondence of the optical property and structure in these materials, which are respectively named as O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA used in the following text.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and optical property of the O- and Y-CDs@PA
ce:section.ce:para: SEM and TEM were first performed to verify the successfully embedding CDs into crystal matrix via observing the morphologies of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA as shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. 4 . Both crystals are irregular bulks with haphazard stack after grinding patiently as shown in the SEM pictures of Fig. S2, but the CDs embedded in the crystal matrixes are not fully identical. Observing the TEM and HRTEM images in Fig. 4, the CDs inside the crystal matrixes are quasi-spherical and inhomogeneous in size, with the average particle size of 2.99 ± 0.25 nm for O-CDs@PA and 1.86 ± 0.12 nm for Y-CDs@PA as illustrated in the bottom right corner of Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b in order. Their lattice fringes are recognized clearly in the HRTEM images at the top right corner of Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b, which are calculated as 0.25 nm and 0.29 nm for O-CDs@PA and 0.25 nm for Y-CDs@PA. Both are respectively corresponded to (100) and (002) planes of the graphite,[33,34] suggesting the graphite-like structure existed in these CDs.

Detailed structural information of the both crystals were further confirmed using the software of Jade 6.5. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the O-CDs@PA was indexed to a phthalimide crystal structure with the standard card of No. PDF#45–1680, implying to form a monomer with the molecular structure of phthalimide model due to the hydrolysis reaction between PA and TU precursors. It is because of the formation of a large number of such monomers that dominate the crystal structure of O-CDs@PA and connects with the CDs inside the crystal. The Y-CDs@PA was also indexed to a phthalimide crystal structure with the standard card of No. PDF#31–1792. Compared to the monomer of O-CDs@PA, the most striking feature is that = NH group is further dehydrogenized to form = N-R group (R represents uncertain elements or groups) in the monomer of Y-CDs@PA, which suggests the intensification of hydrothermal reaction with the temperature increased from 160 ℃ and 220 ℃. Moreover, the CDs inside the crystal are inevitably teared and the core inside the CDs is also bound to collapse in view of the interface effect of this group, which is agreed with the result of TEM. TG/DTG analysis was employed to study the thermodynamic behavior as shown in Fig. 5b, which is helpful to be further insight into the structural change of the both crystals. TG curves of both crystals undergoes roughly the same decomposition process, in which O-CDs@PA is beginning to decompose at 107 ℃ and ended at 230 ℃, yet Y-CDs@PA is at 160 ℃ and at 260 ℃ respectively, suggesting that Y-CDs@PA appears higher thermal stability. The degree of thermal degradation of both crystals can be quantitatively estimated using the peak in the DTG curves, which indicates two-stage degradation for O-CDs@PA and dense multi-stage degradation for Y-CDs@PA. For the former, the first weight loss at around 130 °C is only 3.2 %, being almost negligible. The second weight loss of 96.3 % at 220 °C is attributed to the dehydrogenation and carbonization of functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino on the surfaces of the CDs[35,36], undoubtedly, which should be mainly related to the = NH group inside phthalimide monomer to dehydrogenate with the temperature up to the target value. The weight losses of Y-CDs@PA are 44.9 % at 239 °C, 15.7 % at 243 °C, 10.8 % at 249 °C and 25.9 % at 256 °C successively. Given that these temperature values are slightly above the second weight loss of O-CDs@PA, but below 265 °C, they are possibly caused by the decomposition of amino[37]. Here, =N-R group inside the phthalimide monomer is the greatest possibility to undergo such variation because of the various possibilities of the = N-R groups resulted from the R group, which leads to appearing compact degradation for Y-CDs@PA in 239–256 °C.

To work out the specific form of the = N-R group as detailed as possible, chemical compositions of the O- and Y-CDs@PA were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 5c, the full XPS spectra of both crystals exhibited the presence of C, O, N and S elements, and the corresponding characteristic peaks were at 284.5, 531.8, 399.7, and 164.2 eV, respectively. Comparing the contents of these elements in both crystals, C element is a sharp increase from 62.47 % of O-CDs@PA to 81.90 % of Y-CDs@PA, but all of the other three elements are drastically reduced, especially the N and S elements which are extremely decreased to around one-fifth of the O-CDs@PA (N: 16.07 %) in the Y-CDs@PA (N: 3.10 %), which also can be roughly observed in the SEM pictures of Fig. S2. In the detailed scans of these elements (Fig. 5d-g for O-CDs@PA and Fig. 5h-k for Y-CDs@PA), the C 1 s spectra can be fitted well with four peaks as shown in Fig. 5d and h, assigned to C = C/C–C (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N/C–S (286.4 eV), N-C = O (288.3 eV) and O-C = O (289.0 eV) bonds in turn. As listed in Table 1 , the contents of N-C = O and O-C = O bonds have almost halved the proportion and the content of C–O/C–N/C–S bond has multiplied from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA, suggesting to undergo intensely hybridization using O, N and S elements via the dehydrogenation inside the phthalimide monomer. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 5e and i) are easy to deconvolute into three peaks, which are attributed to C = O (531.6 eV), O-C = O (532.0 eV) and C-O-C/C-O–H (533.2 eV) bonds, and the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 5f and j) also can be deconvoluted into three peaks including pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), pyridinic N (399.9 eV) and graphite N (400.4 eV) bonds, respectively. Furthermore, the contents of both C = O and graphite N bonds present a sharp fall, yet the contents of pyrrolic N bond is drastically increased in Y-CDs@PA as listed in Table 1, indicating that the phthalimide monomers on the surface of CDs are broken down and more phthalimide monomers after the dehydrogenation and hybridization are again formed along with the structural change from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA. Although the content of S element is minimal in both crystals, the S 2p spectra (Fig. 5g and k) contain two components that can be assigned to C-S-C (163.4 eV) and C-SOx (168.7 eV) bonds, and both bonds unexpectedly switched their related intensities from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA, i. e., the C-S-C bond is dominant in O-CDs@PA, which shifts to the C-SOx bond in Y-CDs@PA as shown in Table 1. As a conclusion, these results clearly demonstrate that both crystals have similar phthalimide structure and tend to differ in the size and surface functional groups of the CDs due to the regulation of the phase transformation from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA.

The optical properties of O-CDs@PA and Y-CDs@PA were synthetically explored as shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption spectra of both crystals appear four peaks very similar in the wavelength region of 250–450 nm, only red-shifted integrally beginning from ca. 270 nm for Y-CDs@PA. In detail, the absorptions (named as π, π* in Fig. 6a) less than 270 nm in both crystals can be attributed to the π-π* transition of sp 2-hybridized carbon nanodomains of graphite-like flakes embedded in a matrix comprising of sp 3-hybridized carbon[38,39], and overlap each other. The others are labeled as I1, I2 and I3 at the corresponding position of the peak in the absorption spectrum of O-CDs@PA and I4, I5 and I6 in Y-CDs@PA as shown in Fig. 6a, which belong to the n-π* transition of –COOH, –NH, carboxylic C = O, C = N/C = S and conjugated C-N/C-S etc.[14,40–42] in the form of phthalimide structure or functional group at the interface between CDs and crystal matrix. It is evident that these groups are affected by the change of crystal structure and become more stable in Y-CDs@PA, which leads to red-shifted slightly compared with the spectrum of O-CDs@PA due to the relatively low metastable energy level for Y-CDs@PA. However, besides for a red-shifted similar to the absorption, the excitation spectrum of Y-CDs@PA is improved with higher excitation probability in the wavelength region corresponding to all of the n-π* transition modes except for that corresponding to the π-π* transition mode after undergoing the structural change, providing the most direct evidence to achieve high FLQY for Y-CDs@PA. In fact, the FLQYs of both fluorescent crystals are 22 % for O-CDs@PA and 68 % for Y-CDs@PA after the test, respectively, which fully verifies the pivotal role for the phase transformation to improve the fluorescence efficiency of Y-CDs@PA material. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 3c that both fluorescent crystals exhibited similar emission spectrum with the future of multi-peaks (586, 633, 696 nm for O-CDs@PA and 555, 596, 647 nm for Y-CDs@PA under the optimal excitation) and large stokes shift[43], suggesting to exist multiple chromophores in these materials. But all of the peaks in the emission spectrum of Y-CDs@PA surprisingly undergo a blue shift with respect to the red-shift in the absorption and excitation spectra of both crystals, which implies to have complex ground energies in both crystalline composites. Furtherly, the variations of these peaks depending on the excitation were also analyzed via the 3D fluorescence spectra as shown in Fig. 6b and 6c. There is no doubt that the peaks with the center located at 586 and 633 nm for O-CDs@PA and 555 and 596 nm for Y-CDs@PA (guided with the vertical white dotted lines) appear very dramatic excitation-independent PL feature, yet each peak evolves along with the excitation to present distinct rules. For O-CDs@PA as shown in Fig. 6b, the peak at 586 nm remains constant and starts to get fat with the excitation wavelength up to 295 nm (guided with the horizontal white dotted lines), which again returns to stability at the excitation wavelength of around 310 nm (guided with the horizontal white dotted lines). Coincidentally, the peak at 633 nm suddenly becomes fat almost at the same excitation wavelength of 310 nm after having remained constant with the increasing excitation wavelengths. Thereby, these phenomena illustrate that the centers of luminescence located at 585and 633 nm are dated from the radiative recombination in all excited state of π, π* and I1-I3 energies with different proportion. In addition, the peak at around 696 nm is very weak and seem to be only related to the excitations of I1 and I2 energies. For Y-CDs@PA as shown in Fig. 6c, the peak at 555 nm is also emitted from the radiative recombination in all excited state of π, π* and I4-I6 energies with more uniform proportion. The optimal excitation of the peak at 596 nm is shortened to ca. 300 nm in low wavelength region (guided with the horizontal white dotted lines), suggesting to reduce the contribution of π, π* energy to this luminescent emission. Furthermore, the peak at 647 nm is inherently weak and the corresponding excitation wavelength range is further shortened in high wavelength region. Therefore, the emitting from 647 nm is attributed to the excited state of I4 and I5 energies. The time-resolved PL decay curves of both crystals were also measured at the emission of 586, 633, 696 nm for O-CDs@PA and 555, 596, 647 nm for Y-CDs@PA. The results (Fig. 6d and Table 2 ) show that they (586 and 633 nm for O-CDs@PA, 555 and 596 nm for Y-CDs@PA) can be fitted by a tri-exponential formula[44,45], involving two short-lived components of τ1 and τ2 and a long-lived component τ3, which are ascribed to the recombination processes in the surface states (interface between CDs and crystal matrix) and core state, respectively[46]. The other emissions of 696 nm for O-CDs@PA and 647 nm for Y-CDs@PA, however, can be fitted by a dual-exponential formula, only involving two short-lived components, which is agreed with the results as shown in Fig. 6b and c. Comparing the data of both crystals in Table 2, τ3 with the proportions of 1.70 % and 1.87 % in O-CDs@PA are dramatically increased to 15.87 % and 9.39 % in Y-CDs@PA correspondingly, illustrating that the core sate of π, π* energy has a greater contribution to the fluorescent emitting of Y-CDs@PA. The proportions of τ1 are absolutely dominant for the emission in both crystals, especially the O-CDs@PA that is 61.61 % and 83.14 %, which is assigned to I2 and I5 energies respectively with regard to the strongest absorption and excitation in corresponding crystal composite as exhibited in Fig. 6a. The proportions of τ2 from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA are significantly increased to rival that of the τ1 in Y-CDs@PA, coinciding with the change of the excitation corresponded to the ranges of I1 and I4 energies, respectively. Unfortunately, it does not reflect the contributions of the I3 and I6 energies to fluorescence lifetimes in these data due to the weak emitting from the excitation induced by these energies, but the both energies were indeed beneficial to the fluorescence production as shown in Fig. 6b and c.

On the basis of above analyses on many levels, we speculate on the micro-structural change and luminescence mechanism from O-CDs@PA to Y-CDs@PA under the regulation of temperatures as shown in Fig. 7 . It is observed that O-CDs@PA (left side of Fig. 7) shifts to Y-CDs@PA (right side of Fig. 7) after principally undergoing the core collapse of the CDs and the transformation from –NH to -NR bonds in phthalimide structure at the interface between CDs and crystal matrix, resulting in the change of fluorescent color from orange to yellow and increased FLQYs. According to the literature on Frank-Condon principle[47], the quantized vibration of the nucleus generates a large number of vibrational energy levels in S0 (named as V = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5) and S1 (named as I = 1, 2, 3 in O-CDs@PA, I = 4, 5, 6 with slightly down orderly in Y-CDs@PA) states, which provides various possibilities for the excitonic transitions. In view of the excitation spectra of both crystals, the transition stemmed from I2 energy level is the most probable Frank-Condon transition for O-CDs@PA[48,49], but the transition come from I4 and I5 energy levels are the most probable for Y-CDs@PA due to nearly equal excitation strengths as shown in Fig. 6a, which fundamentally provides a basis to achieve high FLQY in Y-CDs@PA. The π–π* transition at ca. 270 nm is originated from the energy level transition between S0 and S2. The excited electron fall on the S1 energy level by means of the internal conversion (IC) during the transition, and then return to S0 energy level via the radiative emission transition approaches between S0 and S1 energy levels after the vibration relaxation (VR). Therefore, it is understandable that the excitation of around 270 nm gives a portion of contribution to the multi-peak emissions with different proportions for both crystals as illustrated in Fig. 7 (guided with gray forked arrows). Together with the result of fluorescence lifetimes listed in Table 2, more contribution from the S2 energy level to the fluorescent emission is another reason why Y-CDs@PA emits the fluorescence with higher FLQY.

Recently, CDs-based fluorescence materials were frequently employed to fabricate LED devices in light of the multicolor and high FLQYs in solid-state,[50–52] which mainly is by means of both pathways, photoluminescence (PL) and electroluminescence (EL). Because the CDs@PA materials synthesized in this work exhibited excellent afterglow property and photostability (Fig. S3), especially the multi-peaks characteristic of both crystals which endow greater advantage as phosphors to manufacture White Light-Emitting-Diode devices (WLED). It is noteworthy that blue phosphor (B-CDs@PA) used here was again synthesized according to the method explained at the end of part 2.2 in Experimental Section , and the PL spectra are exhibited in Fig. S4. As an attempt, these fluorescent materials firstly were prepared into the CDs@PA@PVP films, using the CDs@PA powders after grinding carefully to pour quantificationally into PVP solutions (detailed description in the part 2.3 of Experimental Section ), and the resultant films presented great dispersibility of the CDs@PA powders and stable fluorescence emissions after exciting in a LED chip (Fig. S5-S7), thereby, providing a possibility for these materials as phosphors to be fabricated into a light converter of the LED device. To achieve the more excellent optical performance, these powders mixed repeatedly without commercial phosphors to realize really total CDs-based WLED according to the trichromatic theory. Finally, three of them were chosen and named as 1-WLED: B-CDs@PA: O-CDs@PA = 5: 1, 2-WLED: B-CDs@PA: Y-CDs@PA = 5: 1, and 3-WLED: B-CDs@PA: Y-CDs@PA: O-CDs@PA = 10: 1: 1, using a UV chip with the wavelength of 365 nm in all of the WLED devices.

As shown in Fig. 8 a-c, optical photographs of the three WLED at on-state are exhibited at right top corner as the inset, respectively and their EL spectra clearly display a lot of characteristic peaks including B-, Y-, O-CDs@PA as well as the chip in corresponding WLED devices, likewise, confirming that resultant powders are stable under current excitation in these WLED devices. At a current of 10 mA, 1-WLED obtains the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinate of (0.37, 0.35) with a color rendering index (CRI) of 88.5 and a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4029 K (Fig. 8d), which endows the WLED device with the features of warm-white light and high CRI and is superior to other CDs-based WLED devices.[43,44] To explore the stability under the current excitation, EL spectra of the 1-WLED with increasing currents from 10 mA to 120 mA were measured as shown in Fig. 8g. All of the peaks are positively related to the currents, but the CRI and CCT are totally contrary and rapidly decrease with the currents up to 60 mA, then almost constant with increasing currents (the inset in Fig. 8g). Fortunately, the CRI still is maintained at 84–85 (＞70, suitable for lighting) although the CCTs become very low which can affects clarity to light using this WLED at the current beyond 60 mA. At the same current of 10 mA, the CIE coordinate of the 2-WLED is (0.32, 0.40) with CRI = 69.4 and CCT = 5849 K, suggesting to be a cold-white light (＞4500 K) and unsuitable for lighting as shown in Fig. 8e. With increasing currents from 10 mA to 120 mA, the EL spectral variation of the 2-WLED is coincided with that in 1-WLED, however, both colorimetric parameters tend to be feasible as the variations of CRI and CCT undergo an intersecting with the current only up to 20 mA, and the CCT continues to decrease to ca. 5075 K when the current increases to 120 mA (the inset in Fig. 8h). For the 3-WLED, CIE coordinate is (0.34, 0.36) with CRI = 87.4 and CCT = 5167 K at the current of 10 mA (Fig. 8f), which is the closest to standard white light of (0.33, 0.33) in three WLED devices. More interestingly, although the EL spectra change at the current excitation of 10–120 mA with the same trend as above WLED devices (Fig. 8i), the CRI and CCT decline synchronously with the current up to ca. 60 mA, then maintain stable with increasing currents to 120 mA. Finally, the CRI is ca. 84 and the CCT is ca. 4150 K (the inset of Fig. 8i), which is warm-white light and more suitable for lighting compared with that at low currents (＜60 mA). Based on the above, 3-WLED device achieved optimal colorimetric parameters of the CRI and CCT at the work current beyond 60 mA, endowing these fluorescence materials with an enormous potential to fabricate WLED device and be used in the field of illuminating engineering as well as information technology.

In summary, a one-step synthesis approach for the composites formed from the carbon dots coated into crystalline matrix (i.e., B-, O-, and Y-CDs@PA) has been developed via the solvothermal reaction of Phthalic acid and Thiourea with the MeOH solvent. After in-depth characterization analysis, we reveal that the transformation of crystal structure induced by the reaction temperature plays critical role for activating the multicolor fluorescence with the feature of multi-peaks and high FLQYs, which not only shifted the crystal structure of the matrix through the phase transformation with the temperature up to 220 ℃, but also effectively regulated the size of the core and the interface between CDs and the matrix, finally, leading to a series of variations of efficiency concerning the radiative recombination between different energy levels. In view of the outstanding optical properties, these fluorescence materials were further applied into the White Light-Emitting-Diode (WLED) devices. As the phosphors without mixed with commercial ones, the fabricated WLED devices obtained excellent photometric parameters, especially the 3-WLED with optimal CRI of 84 and CCT of 4150 K could be as a warm-white light to industrially develop. This discovery will undoubtedly give insight into the fluorescent-emitting influence of crystal as a matrix in CDs-based materials and broaden their practical applications.

Fengli Xu: Data curation. Ruoyu Dong: Data curation. Junchao Cui: Data curation. Yufei Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Weijie Ren: Writing – review & editing. Kai Song: Resources. Shuai Meng: Methodology. Rui Zhang: Software. Kun Li: Investigation. Jingjing Bai: Data curation. Zhenxing Qin: .

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123958.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
